text.title: Required strict flags for TypeScript
text.summary: Five compiler flags every tsconfig.json in a serious TS project must turn on, and what each one catches.

section.title: The required flag set
section.p: The minimal strict configuration is a tight set: `strict` is the umbrella, plus three extras that catch the failure modes LLMs most often introduce, plus `isolatedDeclarations` for libraries.

rule.statement: Every tsconfig.json sets `compilerOptions.strict: true`, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true`. Library tsconfigs additionally set `isolatedDeclarations: true` and `composite: true`.
rule.rationale: `strict` enables noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, strictFunctionTypes, etc. — the baseline. `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` forces narrowing on `arr[i]` and `obj[key]`, the #1 LLM blind spot. `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` separates "missing key" from "key set to undefined", killing partial-update bugs. `isolatedDeclarations` requires explicit return types on every export so non-tsc tools can emit `.d.ts`.

example.note: One base config; per-package configs extend this and add `composite` / `isolatedDeclarations` if they are libraries.
Implicit any everywhere; unchecked array access never narrows; LLM diffs compile but crash at runtime.

antipattern.description: Disabling strict for one file via `// @ts-nocheck` instead of fixing the type error.
antipattern.reason: The escape hatch turns an entire file into untyped JS. Use `// @ts-expect-error: <ticket>` on the single line, or fix the type properly. Agents will copy `@ts-nocheck` patterns once they see one example.

section.reference: Reference base tsconfig with all required flags.
